## - FINITE-TIME CONVERGENCE AND SAMPLE COM - PLEXITY OF MULTI-AGENT ACTOR-CRITIC REIN FORCEMENT LEARNING WITH AVERAGE REWARD
Hairi and Jia Liu Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering The Ohio State University Columbus, OH 43210, USA hairi.1@osu.edu, liu@ece.osu.edu
Songtao Lu IBM Research AI IBM Thomas J. Watson Research Center Yorktown Heights, NY 10598 songtao@ibm.com

## ABSTRACT

In this paper, we establish the first finite-time convergence result of the actor-critic algorithm for fully decentralized multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) problems with average reward. In this problem, a set of N agents work cooperatively to maximize the global average reward through interacting with their neighbors over a communication network. We consider a practical MARL setting, where the rewards and actions of each agent are only known to itself, and the knowledge of joint actions of the agents is not assumed. Toward this end, we propose a mini-batch Markovian sampled fully decentralized actor-critic algorithm and analyze its finite-time convergence and sample complexity. We show that the sample complexity of this algorithm is O(N[2] /ǫ[2] log(N/ǫ)). Interestingly, this sample complexity bound matches that of the state-of-the-art single-agent actorcritic algorithms for reinforcement learning.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

1) Background and Motivations: In recent years, multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) has found a wide range of applications in networked large-scale systems, such as power grid systems (Riedmiller et al., 2000), autonomous driving (Yu et al., 2019; Shalev-Shwartz et al., 2016) and strategic games (Silver et al., 2018; Foerster et al., 2018), to name just a few. Although empirical successes of MARL applications have been widely observed, the fundamental theoretical understanding of how to develop fast-converging and low sample-complexity MARL algorithms, two of the most important performance metrics for MARL, remains in its infancy so far (see, e.g., (Zhang et al., 2021a) for an excellent survey). In particular, two important aspects of cooperative MARL algorithm designs deserve special attention:

- First, in the multi-agent collaborative setting, the information structure (i.e., the assumptions of who have the knowledge of what) is far more complex than its single-agent counterpart and care must be taken in MARL problem formulations. In the cooperative MARL literature so far, many existing works assume full knowledge of joint states and joint actions, which often do not hold true in practice. For example, in autonomous driving (Yu et al., 2019), each vehicle can only observe/detect the actions of the surrounding vehicles that are within its communication range. As another example, in power grid networks (Riedmiller et al., 2000), each power distributor generally does not know the resistor values set by other distributors.

- Second, most MARL theoretical studies in the MARL literature are focused on the discounted total reward setting, where a hyperparameter γ ∈ (0, 1) is introduced as the discount factor in the objective function. Although the discounted total reward setting captures the important aspect of diminishing return in the future, it may not be appropriate for many other applications where the long-term average reward is of interest. For example, in the optimization of distributed communication networks with MARL, the typical and natural performance metrics are long-term average throughput or latency in the steady-state.
The lack of a fundamental understanding on how to develop efficient cooperative MARL algorithms that consider the above two aspects in terms of information structure, scalability, and communication

and sample complexities motivates us to fill this gap by developing a fully decentralized cooperative MARL algorithm in the average reward setting, without assuming joint action knowledge.

2) Technical Challenges: Developing a fully decentralized cooperative MARL algorithm for the average reward setting without full joint action knowledge is highly non-trivial and several major technical challenges naturally arise. First, it is well-known that, even in the single-agent reinforcement learning (RL) setting, the average reward setting is more challenging to analyze compared to the discounted reward setting, which necessitates different proof techniques (Tsitsiklis & Van Roy, 1999). In MARL, the decentralized nature and the lack of joint action-state information further complicate the algorithm design and analysis in the average reward setting. Second, due to the lack of joint action knowledge, the communication costs among agents in MARL will be significantly increased to achieve a satisfying performance, which implies that low sample and communication complexities are even more challenging and critical for MARL without joint action information.

3) Main Results and Contributions: The main contribution of this paper is that we overcome the aforementioned challenges and develop a consensus-based Markovian sampled decentralized actorcritic algorithm for MARL. Our key results in this paper are summarized as follows:

- We propose a batch-sampled actor-critic algorithm that uses consensus updates in TD-sharing among agents. This batch sampling approach enables more efficient communication compared to the classical fully decentralized MARL. Specifically, in order to converge to an ǫ neighborhood of the stationary, we require O(ǫ[−][1] log(ǫ[−][1] )) rounds of communication while only needing O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )) samples. By contrast, the state-of-the-art MARL requires a communication round per sampling. Also, our algorithm allows the use of constant step-sizes in both the actor and the critic steps.

- We provide the first-ever sample complexity analysis in the MARL average reward problem setting without joint action information. Our obtained complexity is O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )), where ǫ is the closeness to the neighborhood of stationary point (treating network size N as a fixed constant). It is worth noting that the order-wise sample complexity of our algorithm matches that of the state-of-the-art single-agent RL algorithms.

## 2 RELATED WORK

In this section, we provide a quick overview on the closely related work on MARL algorithms and their theoretical results, along with several notable related counterparts in single-agent RL.

1) MARL Theoretical Analysis and Algorithm Design: For recent advances in MARL algorithms and their theoretical results, Zhang et al. (2021a) provided a comprehensive survey. Also, Lee et al. (2020) highlighted the evolution from single-agent to multi-agent RL from a distributed optimization perspective. In the broader area of MARL, a line of research has been focused on the MARL policy evaluation problem. These works analyzed the convergence Doan et al. (2019) and proposed various variance reduction of policy evaluation in decentralized MARL algorithms Zhang et al. (2021b). Doan et al. (2019) used i.i.d. sampling and has shown the sample complexity of O[˜] (ǫ[−][1] ) for their TD(0) learning algorithm to reach a mean-square error convergence. However, these algorithms do not involve policy improvement and solely focus on the performance evaluation of given policies. In the areas of joint policy evaluation and improvement, Foerster et al. (2018) considered multi-agent actor-critic algorithm that has a centralized critic and decentralized actors, which is different from our fully decentralized actor-critic algorithm. In contrast, Zhang et al. (2018) established asymptotic convergence results for fully decentralized MARL actor-critic algorithms. Concurrent with our work, Chen et al. (2021) has recently studied the mini-batch Markovian sampling actor-critic algorithm for a class of discounted reward MARL problems, where the finite-time convergence result is obtained. They have applied batch sampling for both actor and critic steps and achieved a sample complexity of O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )), which is the same as ours. We note that, together with our work, these are the first finite-time convergence results for MARL. However, there are several key differences between our work and (Chen et al., 2021). First, we focus on the average reward problem, while Chen et al. (2021) studied the discounted reward setting. Second, in (Chen et al., 2021), agents share a noisy version of the rewards with the neighbors, which requires a re-sampling process from every sampled reward instance. In contrast, we allow agents to share local TD-errors with their neighbors and no re-sampling is required.

2) Related Literature in Single-Agent RL: We note that single-agent RL can be viewed as a centralized approach, where a central controller collects joint actions, rewards and even designs policies for agents. For the single-agent average reward setting, Tsitsiklis & Van Roy (1999) first analyzed the asymptotic convergence of TD(λ) algorithm with function approximations in the policy evaluation problem. Also, Tsitsiklis & Van Roy (2002) provided insights in terms of differences and connections between average reward and discounted reward of TD-based learning algorithms with function approximations. Recently, Qiu et al. (2021) analyzed the sample complexity for an actorcritic algorithm for the average reward problem. In their actor-critic algorithm, they used batch sampling for the critic learning and i.i.d. sampling for the actor step, with sample complexity being O(ǫ[−][3] log[2] (ǫ[−][1] )). By applying mini-batch sampling update, we are able to improve the sample complexity by a factor of O(ǫ[−][1] ). Another closely related work on single-agent RL is (Xu et al., 2020), where the authors studied the discounted reward problem. They used batch sampling for both actor and critic steps in their actor-critic algorithm and developed a new technique to handle bias error in the critic step, which we also adopted for the average approximation parameter analysis in our critic step. This achieved the state-of-the-art sample complexity of O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )) for singleagent RL. In addition, the global convergence of actor-critic algorithm to the optimal policy has been studied in the case of discounted setting with single time scale in (Fu et al., 2020) and linear quadratic regulator in (Yang et al., 2019). However, we note that these settings are fundamentally different from the average reward setting and it will be an interesting future direction to consider global convergence possibility in the average reward setting.
To conclude this section, we summarize the aforementioned related actor-critic and TD algorithms and their sample complexity results in Table 1.

## 3 MULTI-AGENT REINFORCEMENT LEARNING WITH AVERAGE REWARD

## 3.1 SYSTEM MODEL

Consider a multi-agent system with N agents, denoted by N = {1, · · · , N }, operating in a networked environment. Let E be the edge set for a given network G = (N , E). To formulate our MARL problem and facilitate our subsequent discussions, we first define the notion of networked multi-agent MDP as follows.

Definition 1 (Networked Multi-Agent MDP). Let G = (N , E) be a communication network that connects N agents. A networked multi-agent MDP is defined by following tuple (S, {A[i] }i∈N , P, {R[i] }i∈N , G), where S is the global state space observed by all agents, A[i] is the action set for agent i, and P : S ×A×S → [0, 1] is a global state transition function, and R[i] : S ×A is the local reward function for agent i. Let A =[�] i∈N[A][i][ be the joint action set of all agents.]

In this paper, we assume that the global state space S and action space for agent A[i] are finite. As a result, the joint action space A is also finite for finite N . We also note that at time t ≥ 0, all agents can observe the current global state st. However, agent i can only observe its own action a[i] t[∈A][i][, which is the key difference between our model and that in (Zhang et al., 2018), where it is] assumed that the joint actions are observable to all agents. Moreover, each agent can only observe its own reward rt[i][,][i.e.,][agents][do][not][observe][or][share][rewards][with][other][agents][at][time][t][.][The]

## 4 A CONSENSUS-BASED ACTOR-CRITIC ALGORITHM

In this section, we propose a consensus-based actor-critic algorithm that includes two key steps: actor and critic. In the critic step, the algorithm evaluates the value functions for the policy πθt at time t. After the critic step, the algorithm enters the actor step, which improves the policy parameter θt according to the direction from policy gradient as shown in Theorem 1. In both steps, we use constant step-sizes and adopt batch sampling.

In this paper, we use linear function approximations for the value functions. Specifically, each agent i has a parameter w[i] ∈ R[K] to approximate the global value functions Vθ(s; w[i] ) for each state s ∈S. For linear approximation, we have Vθ(s; w[i] ) = φ(s)[T] w[i] , where φ(s) ∈ R[K] denotes the feature for state s. As a result, the gradient of value function at state s with respect to approximation parameter w[i] is φ(s), i.e. ∇wVθ(s; w[i] ) = φ(s).

1) The Critic Step: The critic step is achieved through its own oracle, which is summarized in Algorithm 1. In the critic step, we allow the agents to communicate the approximation parameters w(s) with their neighbors via the communication network with consensus weight matrix A. For agent i ∈N , the parameter is locally updated by following rules:

## 5 THEORETICAL CONVERGENCE ANALYSIS

In this section, we present the convergence results for both the critic and actor steps in Theorems 2 and 3, respectively. Due to space limitation, we relegate the proofs to the supplementary material.

## 6 EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS

In this section, we conduct experiments to compare our proposed consensus-based TD-sharing MARL algorithm with the most related MARL algorithm 1 in Zhang et al. (2018) that also studied average reward. To our knowledge, this is the only work that is directly comparable to ours. The key difference is that the knowledge of joint action is assumed in Zhang et al. (2018), but not in our work. We vary the system size from N = 6, 10 to 15. The blue curve is our TD-sharing algorithm and the red curve is classical MARL algorithm in Zhang et al. (2018). The curves represent the average results of 10 trials and the 95% confidence intervals are also plotted. For the details, see A.1 in Appendix. The results in Figure 1 show that for different system sizes, both algorithms converge to a reasonable objective value. Note that if we use uniformly random policy as the baseline policy, then the objective values will be around 2 due to the setting of our experiments. All simulation results are above this threshold and our TD-sharing algorithm converge to a better objective value. See Section A.2 for addition experiment results.

## 7 CONCLUSION AND DISCUSSION

In this paper, we studied fully decentralized MARL in average reward setting and proposed a batch-sampled actor-critic algorithm. Our main contribution is to establish the first finite time convergence result for fully decentralized MARL in average reward setting, where the complexity is O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )), which matches that of the state-of-the-art single agent RL. The algorithm reaches such convergence with a better communication efficiency. However, it is still in the preliminary stage of the convergence analysis of the MARL since we only used the vanilla average. The future direction will be how to design a more scalable algorithm in terms of system size.

The motivation is well-articulated, clearly identifying two key gaps in the literature: the unrealistic assumption of full observability of joint actions and the overemphasis on discounted reward formulations. The proposed mini-batch Markovian sampled fully decentralized actor-critic algorithm is well-motivated and introduces a consensus-based mechanism that enables efficient communication and learning under partial observability. The claim of establishing the *first finite-time convergence and sample complexity result* in this setting is a strong one and, if fully substantiated in the complete paper, would represent a notable advancement. The stated sample complexity of $ O(N^2 / \epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon)) $, which matches state-of-the-art single-agent bounds up to network dependence, is particularly impressive and suggests scalability in both sample efficiency and decentralized coordination. However, a major concern lies in the clarity and presentation of the technical content in the provided excerpt—specifically, the use of malformed notation (e.g., “ǫ[−][1]” instead of $\epsilon^{-1}$), inconsistent formatting, and missing definitions (e.g., of the consensus mechanism or the minibatch structure) detract from readability and raise questions about the rigor of the full analysis. Moreover, while the communication efficiency claim of $O(\epsilon^{-1}\log\epsilon^{-1})$ rounds appears strong, the dependence on network size $N$ in the communication complexity is not discussed, which is critical in decentralized settings. There is also a lack of precise problem formulation—such as the network topology, mixing time assumptions of the underlying Markov chain, or how credit assignment is handled without joint actions—that will be essential for validating the theoretical claims. In summary, the paper tackles a highly important problem with promising results, but its impact is currently undermined by presentation issues and incomplete technical exposition; a clearer and more rigorous formulation of the algorithm and assumptions will be essential to ensure the validity and reproducibility of the results.

comments.quote: Let A =[] i∈N[A][i][ be the joint action set of all agents.]
comments.explanation: The symbol '[]' appears in the definition of the joint action set. In LaTeX, this is likely a misrendered '∏' (Cartesian product), as the joint action space is standardly defined as $ A = \prod_{i\in\mathcal{N}} A^{[i]} $. The surrounding notation (e.g., $ \pi(a|s) = \prod_{i\in\mathcal{N}} \pi^{[i]}(a^{[i]}|s) $) confirms that Cartesian product is intended. This is almost certainly an OCR or rendering error, not an author error. The meaning is clear from context: agents choose actions independently from their own action sets, and the joint action is a tuple. The use of 'prod' in the policy definition further confirms this. Therefore, while the symbol is corrupted, the intended meaning is unambiguous. No further issue remains once the OCR error is corrected to $ \prod $.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of Notation in Policy Parameter Definition
comments.quote: each agent i’s local policy can be written as π[i][where][θ][i][∈][R][m][i][denotes][the][parameter.][We][let][θ][≜][[(][θ][1][)][T][ ,][ · · ·][,][ (][θ][N][)][T][ ]][T][∈] θ[i][,] RNi=1[m][i]
comments.explanation: This passage contains a garbled LaTeX render. The intended meaning is that each local policy is $ \pi_{\theta^{[i]}}^{[i]}(a^{[i]}|s) $, with parameter $ \theta^{[i]} \in \mathbb{R}^{m_i} $, and the global parameter is $ \theta = [ (\theta^{[1]})^T , \ldots, (\theta^{[N]})^T ]^T \in \mathbb{R}^{\sum_{i=1}^N m_i} $. However, the current text reads 'π[i][where][θ][i][∈][R][m][i]' and then 'θ[≜][(][θ][1][)][T][ ,][ · · ·][,][ (][θ][N][)][T][ ]][T][∈] θ[i][,] RNi=1[m][i]', which includes a recursive definition where 'θ[i]' appears in the definition of θ. This is clearly a rendering artifact. The symbol 'RNi=1[m][i]' is almost certainly $ \mathbb{R}^{\sum_{i=1}^N m_i} $. The surrounding context (e.g., gradients with respect to θ, joint policy) confirms that θ is the concatenated vector. Thus, this is an OCR issue, not a logical one. Once corrected, the definition is standard and consistent.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Critic Step Update Regarding Batch Use
comments.quote: In the critic step, we allow the agents to communicate the approximation parameters w(s) with their neighbors via the communication network with consensus weight matrix A.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'w(s)' is used, but w is defined as a parameter in $ \mathbb{R}^K $, not a function of s in the usual tabular sense—though in linear function approximation, $ V(s) = \phi(s)^T w $, so w itself is not state-dependent. The notation 'w(s)' might mislead a reader into thinking the parameter is state-specific. However, in the algorithm description, it's clear that each agent maintains a single w[i] ∈ ℝ^K, updated using features φ(s). So this is likely a typo or informal shortening of 'the value parameter used to evaluate state s'. The Algorithm 1 confirms that w[i] is a global vector. Therefore, while the notation 'w(s)' is misleading, it's probably meant to indicate 'the value parameter used in evaluating states', and the context resolves the ambiguity. This is common informal usage in RL papers when discussing value estimation. Hence, not a serious issue.

comments.title: Inconsistent Definition of Value Function Gradient
comments.quote: As a result, the gradient of value function at state s with respect to approximation parameter w[i] is φ(s), i.e. ∇wVθ(s; w[i] ) = φ(s).
comments.explanation: Initially, this statement seems correct given the linear function approximation Vθ(s; w[i]) = φ(s)^T w[i], since in basic calculus the gradient of a linear function φ(s)^T w with respect to w is indeed φ(s). However, the notation ∇wVθ(s; w[i]) is ambiguous: it suggests a gradient with respect to a global w, but each agent i uses its own parameter w[i]. The text assigns ∇wVθ(s; w[i]) = φ(s), which is correct only if the gradient is taken with respect to local parameter w[i], but the subscripting of ∇w lacks the agent index, making it appear as though there is a shared parameter. Given that each agent maintains its own w[i] and updates are local before consensus, this is likely just a notational imprecision. However, the use of ∇w without subscripting may mislead a careful reader into thinking a global shared parameter is used, which contradicts the fully decentralized setting. A better notation would be ∇_{w^{[i]}} V_θ(s; w^{[i]}) = φ(s). While the intent is clear in context (agent-wise parameters are emphasized elsewhere), this ambiguity could cause confusion about whether parameters are shared or decentralized.

comments.title: Unfinished Sentence in Assumption 4 and Missing Definition of Aπθ
comments.quote: This assumption implies the following property: for any policy πθ, the inequality w[T] Aπθ w < 0 holds for any w = 0, where Aπθ is
comments.explanation: The sentence breaks off mid-definition: 'where Aπθ is' — this is clearly a truncation, likely from OCR or formatting. Given that Assumption 4 refers to standard TD-learning with linear function approximation and cites Tsitsiklis & Van Roy (1999) and Qiu et al. (2021), we can infer that Aπθ is meant to be the expected gradient matrix, commonly defined as A_{π_θ} = \mathbb{E}_d[φ(s) (φ(s) - γφ(s'))^T] in discounted settings, but in the average reward setting, A_{π_θ} typically relates to the Bellman evaluation operator in the function space. The paper later references that w^T A_{π_θ} w < 0 and its eigenvalue condition, which suggests A_{π_θ} governs the stability of the TD update. However, without its formal definition, and given its critical role in Assumptions 4 and 5, this omission is problematic. Although experts might reconstruct it, this underspecification affects reproducibility and rigor — especially because average-reward A_{π_θ} differs from the discounted case. This missing definition undermines the ability to verify Assumption 5 or relate it to prior work (e.g., Qiu et al., 2021).

comments.title: Missing Objective Function Expression
comments.quote: Mathematically, this can be written as:
comments.explanation: After stating that the goal is to maximize the global average long-term reward, the authors claim 'Mathematically, this can be written as:' but provide no equation — the formula is missing, likely due to OCR error or page break. In the context, J(θ) is referenced later in Theorem 1 and Assumption 6 as the objective, and from standard average-reward RL, we expect J(θ) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \mathbb{E}\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \bar{r}_t \right] or J(θ) = \mathbb{E}_{d_θ, π_θ}[\bar{r}(s, a)]. The absence of the formal definition here is problematic because while J(θ) is central to the entire analysis, it is never explicitly defined in the provided text. Given that the paper claims novelty in the average reward setting, clarity on the objective is essential. Although it may appear later in the full paper, within this extract, this is a major gap — a reader cannot verify whether the policy gradient theorem or convergence analysis aligns with the claimed objective. This is more than just formatting: key definitions are absent.

comments.title: Ambiguity in TD-error for Actor Step: Local vs Global Estimate
comments.quote: Hence, we have that the expected global TD-error is the advantage function, i.e., E[δt|st, at] = Aθ(st, at). Thus, we can use this global TD-error as an unbiased estimate of the advantage function. For agent i, the local TD-error can be computed as δt[i][=][r] t[i] +1[−][µ][i] t[+][ V][θ][(][s][t][+1][)][ −][V][θ][(][s][t][)][,][where] µ[i] t+1[= (1][ −][α][)][µ][i] t[+][ αr] t[i] +1[.][We also note that][ µ][t][=] N[1] i∈N[µ] t[i][.]
comments.explanation: The passage correctly notes that the global TD-error δt (defined using μt and r̄t) is an unbiased estimate of the advantage function. However, it then shifts to defining per-agent local TD-errors δt[i] based on individual rewards and local estimates μ[i]t. It asserts that 'the networked TD-error is actually the average of the local TD-errors' — but this claim is neither formally stated nor proved in this extract. The issue is whether (1/N) Σ_i δt[i] equals δt, the global TD-error. This requires that the average of individual updates matches the centralized average reward update. While μt = (1/N) Σ_i μ[i]t and r̄t = (1/N) Σ_i rt[i] by definition, the local μ[i]t are updated with individual rewards and hence may introduce agent-wise bias or tracking error due to decentralized averaging. The algorithm’s correctness hinges on agents being able to form an unbiased estimate of the advantage via consensus on TD-errors. However, no justification is given that the average of local TD-errors converges to the global one, especially under Markovian sampling and consensus dynamics. This is a non-trivial claim in decentralized settings and skipping justification risks overclaiming.

comments.title: Incorrect or Ambiguous Definition of Global State-Action Value Function
comments.quote: Next, we define the state-action value function: Qθ(s, a) = E[[][∞] t=0[r][¯][t][+1][−][J][(][θ][)][|][s][0][=][s, a][0][=][a, π][θ][]][,]
comments.explanation: This definition of Qθ(s, a) is problematic: it uses an infinite sum starting from t=0 of (r̄_{t+1} − J(θ)) under policy πθ, conditioned on s0=s, a0=a. However, in the average reward setting, the canonical definition of the differential value function is Q_θ(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\left[ \sum_{t=0}^\infty (\bar{r}_t - J(θ)) \mid s_0=s, a_0=a \right], which this appears to be. But here the sum is written as starting from r̄_{t+1}, which suggests that the first reward used is r̄_1 (at t=0) under condition s0=a0=a, which is standard. However, the indexing is inconsistent: the reward at time t+1 depends on st and at, so E[r̄_{t+1} | st, at] = R̄(st, at), which is fine. The bigger issue is that for infinite-horizon average reward, this sum may not converge without ergodicity and function approximation. Moreover, the expression as written suggests a divergent sum unless J(θ) is exactly the average — but the convention is that this sum converges in a 'differential' sense under stationarity. While experts would recognize this as the standard differential Q-function, the notation with [][∞] and lack of proper brackets suggests a possible OCR error in the summation (likely \sum_{t=0}^\infty). More critically, this form assumes the existence of the infinite sum, which requires ergodicity (given in Assumption 1) but is not explicitly justified. The definition is mostly correct but could mislead a reader unfamiliar with average-reward RL.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of global TD-error in actor step
comments.quote: δt = rt+1 − µt + Vθ(st+1) − Vθ(st)
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned that the definition of the global TD-error δt uses Vθ(st+1) − Vθ(st), which differs from the standard definition in average-reward RL. In the average-reward setting, the canonical TD-error is typically rt+1 − J(θ) + Vθ(st+1) − Vθ(st), where J(θ) is the average reward. Here, µt is used instead of J(θ), and µt is a running estimate of the average reward via exponential smoothing: µt+1 = (1−α)·µt + α·¯rt+1. While this is a common practical approximation, the claim that E[δt|st,at] = Aθ(st,at) (the advantage) depends on µt converging to J(θ). However, in finite iterations with constant step-size α, µt is a noisy estimate and does not equal J(θ), so the expectation E[δt|st,at] = E[rt+1 − µt + Vθ(st+1) − Vθ(st)] includes a bias term due to µt ≠ J(θ). The paper asserts that this gives an unbiased estimate of the advantage, but this is only asymptotically true if µt → J(θ) in expectation. No justification is provided for why the bias vanishes in the analysis, especially since constant α prevents convergence of µt to J(θ). The context shows that µ[i]_t is updated with step-size α in the actor step, and the global µt is the average of local estimates, so it remains a moving target. This undermines the claim of unbiasedness, which is central to the policy gradient approximation. The issue is not just notational — it affects the theoretical justification for using δt as a gradient estimator.

comments.title: Mismatch between local TD-error definition and algorithm implementation
comments.quote: For agent i, the local TD-error can be computed as δt[i]=rt[i]+1−µ[i]t+ Vθ(st+1) −Vθ(st)
comments.explanation: The local TD-error δt[i] is defined using the full value functions Vθ(st+1) and Vθ(st), but these are global value functions — each agent uses its own parameter w[i], so Vθ(s) ≈ φ(s)^T w[i]. The expression Vθ(st+1) − Vθ(st) is not well-defined per agent unless all w[i] are equal, which they are not during learning. In Algorithm 1, the local TD-error is correctly computed as δk,τ[i] ← rk,τ[i]+1 − µ[i]k,τ + φ(s)^T w[i] at next and current states — consistent with linear approximation. But in the actor step description, the text writes Vθ(st+1) − Vθ(st) without specifying whose w is used. This creates ambiguity: does each agent use its own w[i] or some consensus value? The algorithm later uses wt[i] in line 8 of Algorithm 2, which resolves the issue, but the text definition is misleading. A careful reader might infer the use of local parameters, but the notation Vθ(s) suggests a global function, which conflicts with decentralized approximation. This could mislead readers about information sharing — no agent has access to a global Vθ unless parameters are perfectly synchronized.

comments.title: Incorrect consensus update formula in critic step
comments.explanation: The consensus update is written as wk[i]+1 = j∈Ni A(i,j) ˜wk[i], which appears to be a typo. The summation index j is used, but the term inside is ˜wk[i], independent of j. This suggests that either the index is wrong or the term should be ˜wk[j]. Given the context — that agent i updates using a weighted average of neighbors’ parameters — and standard consensus algorithms, the correct form should be wk[i]+1 = j A(i,j) ˜wk[j], where ˜wk[j] is the local update of agent j. This is confirmed in Algorithm 1, line 11: 'wk[i]+1 ← j∈Ni A(i,j) · ˜wk[j]', which matches standard distributed averaging. Thus, the textual description has an OCR or copy-paste error, using ˜wk[i] instead of ˜wk[j]. While the algorithm pseudocode is correct, the textual description is mathematically wrong and would mislead a reader relying only on the prose. However, since the algorithm lines are correct and this appears to be a symbolic typo, the issue does not reflect a conceptual flaw.

comments.title: Claim of O(N²/ε² log(N/ε)) sample complexity conflicts with abstract
comments.quote: Our obtained complexity is O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )), where ǫ is the closeness to the neighborhood of stationary point (treating network size N as a fixed constant).
comments.explanation: In the abstract, the paper claims a sample complexity of O(N²/ε² log(N/ε)), as confirmed in Table 1. However, in Section 3.3, it states that the complexity is O(ε⁻² log(ε⁻¹)) 'treating N as a fixed constant'. This is inconsistent. The latter claim hides the N dependence, which is critical given that N appears in the final bound. While it's not uncommon to treat N as a constant in scaling with ε, the paper explicitly lists N in the final bound and compares across N. The theoretical result in Theorem 2 states KcM = O(N/ε log(N/ε)), which is O(N/ε), but this is for the critic alone. The full actor-critic sample complexity should aggregate over T iterations. The claim in the abstract is stronger (quadratic in N), while the text suggests a weaker dependence. This suggests a possible error in combining critic and actor complexities. Without seeing the full proof, the mismatch between abstract and body is troubling. The abstract version is more plausible given consensus mixing time scales with N. The issue affects credibility of the leading-order term in the main result.

comments.title: Unclear initialization and role of µ[i] in critic and actor steps
comments.quote: Update µ[i]k,τ+1←(1−β)·µ[i]k,τ+β·rk,τ[i]+1
comments.explanation: The variable µ[i]k,τ is used in the critic step as an estimate of the long-term return, updated with step-size β. But in average-reward RL, there is no long-term discounted return — the value function estimates deviations from the average reward. The use of exponential moving average here suggests an estimate of J(θ), similar to the actor step’s µ[i]_t. However, in the critic update, µ[i]k,τ is used in the TD-error δk,τ[i] = rk,τ[i]+1 − µ[i]k,τ + φ(s)^T w[i], which mirrors the actor TD-error. But in standard average-reward TD, the TD-error is rt+1 − η_t + V(st+1) − V(st), where η_t estimates J(θ). So this appears to be consistent. However, the paper never formally defines what µ[i] estimates or how it's initialized. Is it shared across iterations? Reset per policy? The initialization and persistence of µ[i] across critic iterations (which are inner loops) are underspecified. Algorithm 1 uses sk,0 = sk-1,M — the last state — but says nothing about µ[i]k,0. If µ[i]k,0 is not properly initialized, the first batch may use a stale estimate. This is critical for bias control. The method is underspecified in a way that affects reproducibility and potentially the convergence proof.

comments.title: Ambiguity in gossip averaging of TD-errors
comments.explanation: The paper states that δ[˜]t[i] = [A^{tgossip}]_{i} ∆0_t, which suggests the i-th row of A^{tgossip} multiplied by the vector of initial local TD-errors. However, in consensus algorithms, each agent applies A repeatedly to its estimate, resulting in δ[˜]t[i] = [A^{tgossip} ∆0_t]_i, the i-th component of the matrix-vector product. The notation used — [A^{tgossip}]_i ∆0_t — if interpreted as row-times-vector, gives a scalar, but could be misread as selecting only agent i’s influence. The standard interpretation is the latter, but the notation is nonstandard and ambiguous. Combined with the matrix ∆0 defined as a B×N matrix (samples across agents), but later treated as an N-dimensional vector per sample, there is confusion. In Algorithm 2, ∆k+1(:,i) ← ∑j A(i,j) ∆k(:,j), which shows that each agent updates its column (sample vector) using neighbors’ columns. So the gossip operates column-wise, meaning each sample’s TD-errors are averaged across agents independently. But the text describes δ[˜]t,l[i] as the result for sample l, which matches. The concern is that the notation [A^{tgossip}]_i ∆0_t is imprecise — it should be (A^{tgossip} ∆0_t)_i or similar. While the algorithm is clear, the mathematical description is sloppy and may mislead.

comments.title: Inconsistent sample complexity claim in abstract and main result
comments.quote: The sample complexity of O(N[2] /ǫ[2] log(N/ǫ)) matches that of the state-of-the-art single-agent actorcritic algorithms for reinforcement learning.
comments.explanation:
Initially, I was concerned because the abstract claims the sample complexity is $ O(N^2 / \epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon)) $, while later in Theorem 3 and the discussion, the total complexity is said to match state-of-the-art single-agent RL with $ O(\epsilon^{-2} \log(\epsilon^{-1})) $, treating $ N $ as constant. This seems contradictory: how can the $ N^2 $ factor be ignored in comparison? However, the authors clarify in the text after Theorem 3 that they treat $ N $ as a fixed constant when comparing to single-agent results. This is a reasonable simplification for order-wise comparison, especially if the focus is on scaling with $ \epsilon $. Still, the abstract overstates the comparison by saying it 'matches' single-agent complexity without qualifying that $ N $ is treated as constant. A careful reader could be misled into thinking the bound is independent of $ N $, which it is not. The table reinforces this concern: it lists $ O(N^2 / \epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon)) $ for this paper, while single-agent is $ O(\epsilon^{-2} \log(\epsilon^{-1})) $, showing a real $ N^2 $ dependence. Thus, the claim that it 'matches' is misleading without explicit qualification.

The authors correctly derive their bound, and treating $ N $ as constant is a common practice when analyzing multi-agent scaling with precision $ \epsilon $. However, the abstract's comparison lacks this nuance, creating a questionable claim.

comments.title: Incorrect total sample complexity expression in Theorem 3
comments.quote: with a total complexity of (B+ MK_c)T = O( N ǫ[2][ 2][log(] N ǫ))
comments.explanation:
At first glance, I assumed OCR had corrupted the LaTeX: 'O( N ǫ[2][ 2][log(] N ǫ))' clearly seems malformed. The intended expression is likely $ O\left( \frac{N}{\epsilon^2} \log\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon}\right) \right) $ or similar. However, from the statement of Theorem 3, we have:
- $ B = \Omega(1/\epsilon) $
- $ K_c = O(\log(1/\epsilon)) $
- $ M $ is the critic batch size, which from Theorem 2 should scale as $ O(\log(1/\epsilon)/\epsilon) $ to meet the error bound
- $ T = \Omega(1/\epsilon) $

So $ (B + M K_c) T $ involves terms like $ (1/\epsilon + (\log(1/\epsilon)/\epsilon) \cdot \log(1/\epsilon)) \cdot (1/\epsilon) = O(1/\epsilon^2 + \log^2(1/\epsilon)/\epsilon^2) $, which is $ O(\log^2(1/\epsilon)/\epsilon^2) $. But the paper says $ O\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon^2} \log\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon}\right)\right) $. The $ N $ dependence likely comes from the critic complexity. From the critic convergence (Theorem 2 summary), we had $ K_c M = O\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon} \log\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon}\right)\right) $. Then $ M K_c T = O\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon} \log\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\epsilon}\right) = O\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon^2} \log\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon}\right)\right) $, which matches. The actor term $ B T = O(1/\epsilon^2) $, which is dominated by the critic term if $ N $ is not too small. So the total complexity is indeed $ O\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon^2} \log\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon}\right)\right) $. The OCR issue (missing backslashes) is clearly to blame for the mangled notation, not a mathematical error. Context resolves this.

comments.title: Incorrect expression for communication complexity in Theorem 3
comments.quote: the communication complexity is (K_c + t_gossip)T = O( 1 ǫ log( N ǫ)).
comments.explanation: I noticed this while parsing the complexities. The expression $ (K_c + t_{\text{gossip}}) T $ is claimed to be $ O\left( \frac{1}{\epsilon} \log\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon}\right) \right) $. From the theorem: $ T = \Omega(1/\epsilon) $, $ K_c = O(\log(1/\epsilon)) $, $ t_{\text{gossip}} = O(\log(N^3/\epsilon)) = O(\log(N/\epsilon)) $. So $ K_c + t_{\text{gossip}} = O(\log(N/\epsilon)) $, and multiplying by $ T = O(1/\epsilon) $ gives $ O\left( \frac{1}{\epsilon} \log\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon}\right) \right) $, which matches. So the asymptotic scaling is correct. However, the expression $ (K_c + t_{\text{gossip}}) T $ mixes two different types of communication: $ K_c $ refers to iterations in the critic step (each involving consensus), while $ t_{\text{gossip}} $ is the number of gossip rounds *per actor step*. In the algorithm, during each of the $ T $ outer iterations, we run $ K_c $ critic steps (each with one consensus update), and one actor step (with $ t_{\text{gossip}} $ gossip rounds). So total communication rounds should be $ (K_c + t_{\text{gossip}}) T $, which is what's written. So the expression is correct. My initial concern was unwarranted; the authors correctly account for both sources of communication.

comments.title: Misleading claim about communication rounds per sample
comments.quote: we only need a communication round per O(ǫ[−][1]) sampling
comments.explanation: At first, this sounds like a major improvement: only one communication round every $ O(1/\epsilon) $ samples. But the total sample complexity is $ O(N / \epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon)) $, and total communication rounds are $ O(1/\epsilon \log(N/\epsilon)) $. So communication rounds per sample is roughly $ \frac{O(1/\epsilon \log(N/\epsilon))}{O(N / \epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon))} = O(\epsilon / N) $, meaning many samples per communication round, which is indeed good. But the phrasing 'communication round per $ O(1/\epsilon) $ sampling' implies $ O(1/\epsilon) $ samples per communication, which would mean constant communication as $ \epsilon \to 0 $, but in fact communication grows as $ 1/\epsilon $. The correct interpretation is that communication complexity is $ O(1/\epsilon) $, while sample complexity is $ O(1/\epsilon^2) $, so communication is sublinear in samples. The statement is technically accurate if 'per' is read as 'for every', but it's easily misread as claiming fewer communication rounds than is the case. It overstates efficiency without clarifying the $ \epsilon^{-1} $ dependence. However, the rest of the paper correctly presents the asymptotics, so this is a minor framing issue.

comments.title: Undefined approximation error introduced late
comments.quote: Define the approximation error introduced by using linear approximation in the critic step, ξapprox[critic][=] maxθ∈Ri∈N[mi][E][s][∼][d][θ][[][|][V][π][θ][(][s][)][ −][V][w] πθ[∗][(][s][)][|][2][]][.]
comments.explanation: The term $ \xi_{\text{approx}}^{\text{critic}} $ appears suddenly in Theorem 3 without prior definition or motivation in earlier sections. While the concept of approximation error from linear function approximation is standard in RL, this abrupt introduction lacks context: no discussion of when it is small, how it relates to the feature design, or its role in the bias-variance trade-off. It also appears only in the bound $ \mathbb{E}[\|\nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)\|^2] \leq \epsilon + O(\xi_{\text{approx}}^{\text{critic}}) $, implying the algorithm converges to a neighborhood of size depending on this error. This is correct and expected, but the sudden appearance and lack of discussion risks misleading a reader into thinking it can be made arbitrarily small, when in fact it's a structural bias determined by the feature choice. The authors reference prior work where this is standard, but for a self-contained presentation, this definition should appear earlier, perhaps in Section 3.4 or 4. Given its central role in the final bound, it should not be defined only at the point of use.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of O-notation in bit complexity claim
comments.quote: The amount of information (in terms of bits) is (KcNK + BNtgossip)T = O( ǫ[N][2][log][N] ǫ[)][.]
comments.explanation:
This bit complexity expression is problematic. The left-hand side has $ K $, which is undefined — likely a typo for $ M $, the critic batch size. Assuming $ K \to M $, and $ M = O(\log(N/\epsilon)/\epsilon) $ from critic convergence, $ K_c = O(\log(N/\epsilon)) $, $ t_{\text{gossip}} = O(\log(N/\epsilon)) $, $ B = O(1/\epsilon) $, $ T = O(1/\epsilon) $. Then:
- Critic communication: $ K_c N M T = O(\log(N/\epsilon) \cdot N \cdot \log(N/\epsilon)/\epsilon \cdot 1/\epsilon) = O(N \log^2(N/\epsilon)/\epsilon^2) $
- Actor communication: $ B N t_{\text{gossip}} T = O(1/\epsilon \cdot N \cdot \log(N/\epsilon) \cdot 1/\epsilon) = O(N \log(N/\epsilon)/\epsilon^2) $
Total is dominated by critic: $ O(N \log^2(N/\epsilon)/\epsilon^2) $. But the paper claims $ O(N^2 / \epsilon \cdot \log(N/\epsilon)) $, which has $ N^2 $ and $ 1/\epsilon $, missing a $ 1/\epsilon $ factor and having excess $ N $. This does not match. Even if $ M $ is constant, $ K_c N M T = O(N \log(N/\epsilon)/\epsilon) $, still not $ O(N^2 / \epsilon \log(N/\epsilon)) $. The $ N^2 $ suggests each agent sends size-$ N $ messages, but TD-errors are scalar. Each communication round likely involves sending a $ K $-dimensional vector (the critic parameter or TD-error), not size proportional to $ N $. So the per-round cost should be $ O(K) $ or $ O(1) $, not $ O(N) $. Thus, the $ N $ in the bit count should be the number of rounds times per-message cost, not an extra $ N $ multiplier. The claim appears incorrect — it overstates the bit complexity by a factor of $ N $. This is a real error in the communication cost analysis.

comments.title: Inconsistent sample complexity claim in abstract vs. main text and table
comments.quote: We show that the sample complexity of this algorithm is O(N[2] /ǫ[2] log(N/ǫ)).
comments.explanation: The abstract claims a sample complexity of $ O(N^2 / \epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon)) $, which appears to depend on $ N^2 $. However, in the PAPER SUMMARY, the stated total sample complexity is $ (B + M K_c) T = O\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon^2} \log\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon}\right)\right) $, and this is echoed in Table 1 where the entry for 'This paper' is $ O(N^2 / \epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon)) $. There is a discrepancy: the abstract, summary equation, and the table do not align. The PAPER SUMMARY suggests a linear dependence on $ N $, while the abstract and table suggest quadratic. Given that Theorem 3 states $ O\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon^2} \log\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon}\right)\right) $, this implies the table and abstract likely contain a typo—$ N^2 $ should be $ N $. Since sample complexity in decentralized MARL with $ N $ agents often scales linearly with $ N $ when each agent performs local computation, a $ N^2 $ factor would be surprising without justification (e.g., all-pairs communication). The context partially resolves this because the formal equations suggest $ O(N / \epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon)) $, but the repeated presence of $ N^2 $ in the abstract and table indicates a real inconsistency that could mislead readers about scalability. This undermines the comparison claim that it matches single-agent bounds, which are independent of $ N $.

comments.title: Misleading claim about communication complexity advantage
comments.quote: we require O(ǫ[−][1] log(ǫ[−][1] )) rounds of communication while only needing O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )) samples.
comments.explanation: The text states the algorithm needs $ O(\epsilon^{-1} \log(\epsilon^{-1})) $ communication rounds, which matches the PAPER SUMMARY's communication complexity $ (K_c + t_{\text{gossip}}) T = O\left(\frac{1}{\epsilon} \log\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon}\right)\right) $. However, the claim that 'only one communication round per sampling' in prior work is inaccurate. Zhang et al. (2018), cited as state-of-the-art, uses decentralized TD(0) with one communication per sample update, but Chen et al. (2021) (also cited) already uses batching and thus reduces communication frequency. The claim overstates novelty by implying that any algorithm needing one communication per sample is current state-of-the-art, when in fact recent batching methods already improve this. Moreover, the paper's communication complexity depends on both $ K_c $ (number of critic steps before actor update) and $ t_{\text{gossip}} $ (steps to achieve consensus), and while batching reduces $ K_c $, the gossip steps per round $ t_{\text{gossip}} $ scale as $ O(\log(1/\epsilon)) $, so total rounds are $ O(\epsilon^{-1} \log(1/\epsilon)) $, not $ O(\epsilon^{-1} \log(\epsilon^{-1})) $ independently of $ N $. The claim is technically vague but largely resolved by context. Still, the omission of dependence on $ N $ in the abstract’s $ O(\epsilon^{-1} \log(\epsilon^{-1})) $ risks misleading readers about scalability.

comments.title: Unclear origin of constant $ \kappa_3 $ in gossip condition
comments.quote: t_{\text{gossip}} \geq \frac{1}{2 \log(1 - \eta^{N-1})^{-1}} \log\left(\frac{64 \kappa_3 N}{\epsilon}\right)
comments.explanation: The condition for $ t_{\text{gossip}} $ involves a constant $ \kappa_3 $, which is not defined in the PAPER SUMMARY. While constants like this often arise from concentration inequalities or gradient bounds, its absence from the key definitions suggests an omission. The assumptions and prior definitions mention $ \kappa $ (possibly in Lemma 4 or Assumption 5), but $ \kappa_3 $ is not introduced. In collaborative learning, such constants typically depend on mixing times, gradient variance, or network topology. Without definition, a reader cannot assess whether the bound is loose or tight, nor reproduce the algorithm’s parameter tuning. Given that communication complexity is a key contribution, this underspecification affects reproducibility. This is a forward-reference issue, but since Lemma 4 (cited alongside $ R_w $) should define $ \kappa_3 $, and it is missing even in the summary, it qualifies as a real gap.

comments.title: Ambiguity in actor step update due to missing pseudo-gradient aggregation
comments.quote: for l = 0, · · · , B
comments.explanation: The actor update in Algorithm 2 is cut off mid-sentence, but more importantly, the method for aggregating local gradients in the actor step is underspecified. The paper states that agents use the global TD-error as an unbiased estimate of the advantage function, but the global TD-error $ \delta_t = \bar{r}_{t+1} - \mu_t + V_\theta(s_{t+1}) - V_\theta(s_t) $ depends on the global average reward estimate $ \mu_t $, which is computed as $ \mu_t = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i\in\mathcal{N}} \mu_t^i $. However, neither the algorithm nor the summary clearly spells out how $ \mu_t $ is computed via consensus. While the critic step uses gossip on $ w_k^i $, the actor step requires average consensus on $ \mu_t^i $, but this is not detailed. The communication complexity analysis likely assumes this is done efficiently, but without specifying whether this uses the same gossip steps or additional communication, the algorithm lacks clarity. This is a genuine underspecification: readers cannot implement the full algorithm without assumptions about how local $ \mu^i $ are aggregated.

comments.title: Overstatement of 'first finite-time convergence' claim
comments.quote: We establish the first finite-time convergence result of the actor-critic algorithm for fully decentralized multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) problems with average reward.
comments.explanation: The claim of being the 'first' finite-time convergence result for MARL with average reward is contradicted by the RELATED WORK section, which cites Chen et al. (2021) as providing finite-time convergence for MARL (albeit in the discounted setting). More critically, the paper itself acknowledges prior finite-time results in single-agent settings (Qiu et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2020) and notes Chen et al. (2021) achieves finite-time for discounted MARL—so the 'first for MARL' claim is invalid. However, the intended claim is likely 'first for the average reward setting in fully decentralized MARL,' which is plausible and not contradicted by related work. The unqualified 'first' is therefore a hyperbole. Since the context clarifies the novelty lies in the average reward setting, this is not fatal, but it overstates the contribution in a way that could mislead readers unfamiliar with the literature.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a significant gap in the multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) literature by establishing finite-time convergence and sample complexity bounds for a fully decentralized actor-critic algorithm under the average reward criterion. The motivation is well-articulated, specifically in its identification of the limitations of assuming joint action knowledge and the restricted scope of existing discounted reward frameworks. The manuscript is technically ambitious, aiming to replicate the efficiency of single-agent state-of-the-art results within a more constrained, decentralized multi-agent environment. The clarity of the problem formulation is high, and the authors make a compelling case for the novelty of their approach, particularly regarding the use of mini-batch Markovian sampling to optimize communication costs. However, the most significant challenge for the reader will be verifying the rigor of the "consensus-based TD-sharing" mechanism; because the average reward setting lacks the contractive properties of the discounted case, the stability analysis of the coupled actor-critic updates—especially under decentralized constraints—must be handled with exceptional mathematical precision. Overall, the paper demonstrates strong theoretical promise but will ultimately be judged by the robustness of the proof techniques used to manage the interplay between decentralized consensus and average-reward stochastic approximation.

comments.title: Inconsistent sample complexity notation between claims
comments.explanation: There is a systemic inconsistency regarding whether N is treated as a constant or a variable. The Abstract and Table 1 include N^2, whereas Section 1 treats N as a constant, resulting in O(£^{-2} log(£^{-1})). This notation mismatch complicates the comparison of results against external baselines and creates confusion regarding the algorithm's scalability.

comments.title: Use of non-standard and malformed symbols for product/summation
comments.quote: A = ô i∈N A[i] and π(a|s) = ô i∈Nπ[i] ( a[i] |s )
comments.explanation: The character 'ô' is used in place of the operator symbols for the Cartesian product and the product of independent probabilities. Combined with syntax errors in the summation bounds in Algorithm 1, these OCR-induced or typographical errors hinder the mathematical clarity of core definitions.

comments.title: Mathematical errors in Consensus Matrix definition
comments.explanation: The definition of the consensus/mixing matrix A is garbled (OCR artifacts), preventing the reader from confirming the spectral properties ($ho$) required for the convergence proofs later in the paper. The definition must be corrected to the standard form A = I - (1/d_max)L.

comments.title: Conflicting description of the Consensus Update
comments.explanation: The prose description of the consensus update contradicts both Algorithm 1 and standard consensus theory by using the local weight $w_k^i$ inside a summation over neighbors $j$, rather than the neighbor's weight $w_k^j$. This renders the decentralized update rule mathematically incorrect as described.

comments.title: Critical scaling error in policy gradient update expression
comments.explanation: The text defines the policy gradient update using a multiplier 'B' for the batch sum, whereas Algorithm 2 correctly uses '1/B' to take the sample average. Using 'B' would result in a gradient step-size $B^2$ larger than intended, which would likely cause numerical instability and divergence.

comments.title: Malformed convergence bound in Theorem 3
comments.explanation: The expression for the batch size $B$ in Theorem 3 is syntactically broken, specifically regarding the treatment of the spectral gap $ρ$. As written, the condition is mathematically ill-formed and cannot be verified.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a significant gap in the multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) literature by establishing finite-time convergence and sample complexity bounds for a fully decentralized actor-critic algorithm under the average reward criterion. The motivation is well-articulated, specifically in its identification of the limitations of assuming joint action knowledge and the restricted scope of existing discounted reward frameworks. The manuscript is technically ambitious, aiming to replicate the efficiency of single-agent state-of-the-art results within a more constrained, decentralized multi-agent environment. The clarity of the problem formulation is high, and the authors make a compelling case for the novelty of their approach, particularly regarding the use of mini-batch Markovian sampling to optimize communication costs. However, the most significant challenge for the reader will be verifying the rigor of the "consensus-based TD-sharing" mechanism; because the average reward setting lacks the contractive properties of the discounted case, the stability analysis of the coupled actor-critic updates—especially under decentralized constraints—must be handled with exceptional mathematical precision. Overall, the paper demonstrates strong theoretical promise but will ultimately be judged by the robustness of the proof techniques used to manage the interplay between decentralized consensus and average-reward stochastic approximation.

comments.title: Index inconsistency in Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1 line 10, the summand index is τ from 0 to M-1, but the summation symbol ∑ has the bounds τ=0 inscribed and M-1 as a superscript. However, the expression ∑τM=0-1 contains a syntax error where τM appears as a single index or variable. Furthermore, line 8 updates δ using τ, which is consistent, but the summation notation is likely an OCR-induced error for ∑_{τ=0}^{M-1}. While clear to a reader in context, it is technically malformed.

comments.title: Inconsistent sample complexity notation between Abstract and Section 1
comments.quote: We show that the sample complexity of this algorithm is O(N[2] /ǫ[2] log(N/ǫ)).
comments.explanation: The abstract explicitly states a sample complexity of O(N^2/ε^2 log(N/ε)). However, in Section 1 (Main Results and Contributions), the text states: 'Our obtained complexity is O(ε[−][2] log(ε[−][1] )), where ε is the closeness to the neighborhood of stationary point (treating network size N as a fixed constant).' Treating N as a constant to justify omitting it in one section while including it as a variable in the Abstract is confusing and potentially misleading for a reader trying to understand how the complexity scales with the number of agents.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of the Average Reward function
comments.quote: where R[¯] (s, a) = N1 ∑i∈N[R][i][(][s, a][)][is][the][global][average][reward][function.]
comments.explanation: The text defines the global average reward function `R¯` using 'N1', which is clearly an OCR misread for 1/N. While context makes it obvious that this is the arithmetic mean of agent rewards, it is a mathematical error in the presentation of critical equations.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Table 1 regarding sample complexity notation
comments.quote: O(N 2/ǫ2 log(N/ǫ))
comments.explanation: In Table 1, the entry for 'This paper' lists the sample complexity as O(N^2/ǫ^2 log(N/ǫ)). However, elsewhere in the abstract and throughout Section 1, the authors consistently state the sample complexity as O(ǫ^{-2} log(ǫ^{-1})) (treating N as a constant). While including the N dependence is technically more accurate for a MARL paper, the sudden change in notation (using N^2/ǫ^2 instead of 1/ǫ^2 specifically) and the inclusion of N inside the log term creates confusion. A reader comparing this to the 'Chen et al.' entry in the same table (which uses the ǫ-only notation) might be misled into thinking the complexity is qualitatively different or worse than the claims made in the body text.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Joint Action Space definition
comments.quote: Let A = ô i∈N A[i] be the joint action set of all agents.
comments.explanation: The OCR correctly identifies the character ô, but in the context of defining a joint action set A which is a product space of A[i], the intended mathematical symbol is almost certainly the Cartesian product symbol (×). While the context (Definition 1) implies this is a product space, the notation ô is non-standard. Given the instruction to flag symbols that appear inconsistent or malformed if they remain issues, this symbol needs to be addressed as a potential notation error that could confuse a reader unfamiliar with the specific OCR-artifact pattern.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Joint Policy definition
comments.quote: π(a|s) = ô i∈Nπ[i] ( a[i] |s )
comments.explanation: Similar to the entry above, the Greek letter ô is used to represent an operator. For a joint policy $\pi(a|s)$ where actions are independent across agents, the correct operator is the product symbol $\prod$. Representing the product of local policies using ô throughout the definition section is a significant notation error that hinders mathematical clarity.

comments.title: Inconsistent Consensus Update Equation in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: 11 Consensus Update wk[i] +1[←][] j∈Ni[A][(][i, j][)][ ·][w][˜] k[j][;]
comments.explanation: In line 10 of Algorithm 1, the authors calculate a temporary value w˜k[i]. In line 11, the consensus update uses this temporary value, denoted as w˜k[j]. However, the sum is written as an index over Ni (the neighborhood of i). While functionally close to consensus, the notation A(i, j) w˜k[j] is slightly ambiguous regarding whether the weight A(i, j) is applied correctly to the specific neighbors. More notably, it is inconsistent with the textual description provided in Section 4, item 1 (next to Algorithm 1), where it states: 'wk[i] +1[=][ ] j∈Ni[A][(][i, j][) ˜][w] k[i][.'. The index j here is inside the sum, but the variable is written as w˜k[i], which doesn't make sense as it should be the neighbor's value w˜k[j]. Given the context of standard consensus algorithms (e.g., Nedic & Ozdaglar), this is clearly a typo for w˜k[j]. It is a minor notation error that could confuse a reader trying to implement the algorithm.

comments.title: Dimensionality mismatch in weight definition
comments.quote: w[i] ∈ R[K] to approximate the global value functions Vθ(s; w[i] )
comments.explanation: In Section 4, the authors define local parameter w[i] ∈ R[K] for approximating a *global* value function Vθ(s; w[i] ). However, the global value function Vθ(s) is typically a single scalar function. If it is global, agents should theoretically converge to a consensus weight w such that V(s) = φ(s)^T w. By allowing each agent to have its own w[i], the authors seem to implicitly assume that the parameter w[i] converges to the global solution through the consensus mechanism. While mathematically valid in a distributed optimization framework, the phrasing 'approximate the global value function' with a local vector w[i] without explicitly mentioning the eventual consensus to a global w* can be slightly misleading to a reader not familiar with the distributed consensus-to-consensus convergence property.

comments.title: Index confusion in Algorithm 1 description
comments.explanation: In the paragraph following Algorithm 1, the text explicitly writes the update as the sum over j, but uses the index of the current agent 'i' for the value (w˜k[i]) instead of 'j'. This is a contradiction because the sum would simplify to a single value if all terms were w˜k[i], effectively ignoring the neighbors' contributions. Contextually, it is clearly intended to be a sum over j of A(i,j)*w˜k[j]. It is a minor typo that does not undermine the algorithm but is technically incorrect in the text.

comments.title: Inconsistent indexing in consensus update equation
comments.explanation: In Section 4, the consensus update equation states w_{k+1}^i = \sum_{j \in N_i} A_{ij} \tilde{w}_k^i. Based on the preceding algorithmic context and Algorithm 1 (line 11), the authors clearly intend for agent i to aggregate the *neighboring* estimates, i.e., \sum_{j \in N_i} A_{ij} \tilde{w}_k^j. The local update rule shown in the text uses index 'i' for the term \tilde{w}, effectively ignoring neighbors, which contradicts both the definition of consensus and the provided Algorithm 1. While this is likely an OCR error or a typo in the manuscript text, it is mathematically critical to the decentralized nature of the algorithm.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of policy gradient update in text
comments.explanation: The text states the update rule for the policy parameter as θ_{t+1}^i = \theta_t^i + B \alpha \sum_{l=0}^{B-1} \tilde{\delta}_{t,l}^i \cdot \psi_{t,l}^i. However, Algorithm 2 (Line 20) defines this as \theta_{t+1}^i \leftarrow \theta_t^i + \frac{\alpha}{B} \sum_{l=0}^{B-1} \tilde{\delta}_{t,l}^i \cdot \psi_{t,l}^i. The text includes a factor 'B' in the numerator, whereas the algorithm correctly uses '1/B' to represent the sample average of the gradient mini-batch. Using the 'B' multiplier would result in a scaled step-size that is B^2 times larger than the algorithm implementation, which would likely cause divergence.

comments.title: Conflicting definition of global TD-error
comments.quote: At time t, suppose we have samples st, at, st+1 and the rewards {rt[i] +1[}][i][∈N][then][the][advantage] function is as follows: Advθ(st, at) = E[¯rt+1¯ − J(θ) + Vθ(St+1) − Vθ(st)|st, at]
comments.explanation: The expression 'E[¯rt+1¯ − J(θ)...]' contains a double bar/overbar notation ('¯rt+1¯') that is typographically confusing. Given that \bar{r}_{t+1} is defined as the average reward, the second bar is likely an OCR error or a rogue symbol. While a reader can infer the intended meaning (the Bellman error), the notation is inconsistent with the preceding formal definition of the advantage function.

comments.title: Possible Typo in Theorem 3 Convergence Bound Index
comments.explanation: The term involving the spectral gap (rho) appears syntactically broken in the OCR/text. It seems to intend to capture the mixing rate (likely related to the spectral gap \rho of the Markov chain), but the usage of \rho[1] and the bracket structure `(1] -[2][[]` appears nonsensical for a standard convergence bound expression. While the intent is clearly a condition on the batch size B to account for Markovian sampling error, the expression as written is mathematically ill-formed.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Communication Complexity Scaling
comments.quote: And the communication complexity is (Kc + tgossip)T = O(1/ǫ log(N/ǫ)).
comments.explanation: In Section 4 and early section 5, the authors mention that their algorithm improves communication complexity by requiring communication only per batch. However, the expression provided here \(O(1/\epsilon \log(N/\epsilon))\) seems to multiply communication rounds by T. Usually, communication complexity is defined as the total number of bits or total message rounds over the course of the algorithm. Given that T (total iterations) is tied to 1/\epsilon^2, the multiplication by T suggests a much higher complexity than the O(1/\epsilon) per batch. If they mean total communication rounds over the entire run T, it should not be O(1/\epsilon).

comments.title: Potential Index/Variable Mismatch in Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: In step 10 of Algorithm 1, the update uses \(w_k^i\) on the right-hand side. In many standard stochastic approximation updates for actor-critic, one would typically use an averaged parameter or ensure that the batch update is correctly scaled by the learning rate. While \(M\beta\) (or \(\beta/M\) which is more common) is used, the notation here is slightly inconsistent with the definition of \(\delta\) in step 8, which already depends on \(w_k^i\). If \(w_k^i\) is meant to be the iterate for iteration k, the recurrence relation needs to be crystal clear. The notation is likely correct in intent but prone to misinterpretation regarding whether the batch gradient is applied to the starting or running weight.

comments.title: Inconsistency in notation for the critic step consensus update
comments.explanation: In Section 4, the prose states 'wk[i] +1[=][ ] j∈Ni[A][(][i, j][) ˜][w] k[i][.]', while line 11 of Algorithm 1 writes the update as 'wk[i] +1[←][] j∈Ni[A][(][i, j][)][ ·][w][˜] k[j][;]'. The prose omits the index 'j' on the weight w-tilde, whereas the algorithm correctly uses the neighbor's value 'w-tilde_k^j'. Given the consensus-based nature of the algorithm, the prose definition is mathematically incorrect, though the algorithm itself seems to use the correct formulation. The mismatch could confuse a reader trying to reconcile the text with the pseudo-code.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the definition of the communication matrix A
comments.quote: A = 1[−][L][)][,][where][deg][(][G][)][is][the][degree][of][the][graph][G][(i.e.][the][maximal][vertex][degree), I is the identity matrix of conforming dimensionality, and L is the Laplacian matrix of the graph... it is easy to verify that this matrix is symmetric, doubly stochastic and η ≥ deg1(G)[≥] N[1][.]
comments.explanation: The text attempts to define A = I - (1/deg_max)L. However, the OCR/text output is highly garbled ('A = 1[−][L][)]', 'deg(G)[(][deg][(][G][)][ ·][ I] gree)'). While I can infer the authors meant A = I - (1/d_max)L, the expression is mathematically underspecified due to the printing errors. For an academic paper, even in the summary/intro context, the definition of the consensus matrix is a foundational component of the convergence proof provided in Theorem 2 and 3. The garbled text makes the verification of whether A is doubly stochastic and the lower bound of η opaque.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript makes a substantial theoretical contribution by deriving the first finite-time convergence and sample complexity bounds for fully decentralized multi-agent actor-critic reinforcement learning under average reward objectives without requiring joint action knowledge, effectively addressing critical gaps regarding information structure and steady-state optimization. The writing is clear, and the problem formulation is well-motivated, with the adoption of mini-batch Markovian sampling and constant step-sizes adding practical relevance. A notable strength is the sample complexity of $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-2}\log(\varepsilon^{-1}))$, which matches single-agent rates, demonstrating the efficacy of the proposed consensus-based algorithm in overcoming the challenges of decentralization and average rewards. However, a significant issue concerns the dependency on the number of agents $N$; the abstract reports an $\mathcal{O}(N^2/\varepsilon^2)$ complexity, yet the introduction subsequently treats $N$ as a fixed constant, which obscures the algorithm's scalability in large-scale networks and creates ambiguity regarding whether this polynomial dependence constitutes a bottleneck compared to centralized or less restrictive decentralized baselines. Additionally, the claim of novelty must be rigorously distinguished from concurrent work on discounted rewards and asymptotic results to avoid overstatement, and the precise interplay between the claimed communication efficiency and sample complexity bounds should be explicitly reconciled to fully validate the algorithm's advantages over state-of-the-art methods.

comments.title: Inconsistent sample complexity bounds across sections
comments.quote: We show that the sample complexity of this algorithm is O(N[2] /ǫ[2] log(N/ǫ)). ... Our obtained complexity is O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )), where ǫ is the closeness to the neighborhood of stationary point (treating network size N as a fixed constant).
comments.explanation: The abstract and Table 1 state the sample complexity with an explicit N^2 factor, i.e., O(N^2/ε^2 log(N/ε)). However, the second bullet under 'Main Results and Contributions' claims the complexity is O(ε^{-2} log(ε^{-1})) by treating N as fixed. This presents a direct notational and logical contradiction. If the N^2 factor is a critical part of the finite-time bound, it should not be dropped in the contributions bullet without clarification. Conversely, if the algorithm is considered to have the same sample complexity as single-agent RL, the N^2 factor should be explained as a constant overhead or removed from the asymptotic notation in the abstract. As written, it is unclear whether the sample complexity fundamentally scales with N^2 or if N is merely a hidden constant in the order-wise notation.

comments.title: Misleading claim about communication overhead in state-of-the-art MARL
comments.quote: By contrast, the state-of-the-art MARL requires a communication round per sampling.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that state-of-the-art MARL requires one communication round per sample. However, in Section 2, they explicitly discuss Chen et al. (2021) as a concurrent work that 'has applied batch sampling for both actor and critic steps,' which inherently decouples communication from per-sample updates. Claiming that 'state-of-the-art MARL' universally requires one communication round per sample is inaccurate and contradicts the related work section. The statement likely refers to 'classical' or 'online' fully decentralized MARL (e.g., Zhang et al., 2018), but using the term 'state-of-the-art' overstates the claim and creates confusion.

comments.title: Mathematically imprecise terminology for convergence parameter ε
comments.quote: where ǫ is the closeness to the neighborhood of stationary point
comments.explanation: The phrase 'closeness to the neighborhood' is mathematically imprecise and non-standard. In convergence analysis, one is typically either 'close to a stationary point' (distance < ε) or 'within an ε-neighborhood of a stationary point'. 'Closeness to the neighborhood' suggests a nested relationship that doesn't align with standard optimization criteria. This phrasing could confuse readers about what ε actually measures in the convergence bound.

comments.title: Typographical notation error regarding value function parameter
comments.quote: In the critic step, we allow the agents to communicate the approximation parameters w(s) with their neighbors via the communication network with consensus weight matrix A.
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'approximation parameters w(s)', implying the parameter depends on the state s. However, earlier in Section 4, the value function is defined as V_θ(s; w[i]) = φ(s)^T w[i], where w[i] ∈ R^K is a fixed global parameter vector for the linear approximation, independent of s. The '(s)' is likely a typo and should simply be 'w[i]' or 'w'. This minor notation error could mislead readers into thinking the approximator is state-dependent when it is actually a global feature weight vector.

comments.title: Typo in Assumption 4 invalidates the negative definiteness condition
comments.quote: This assumption implies the following property: for any policy πθ, the inequality w[T] Aπθ w < 0 holds for any w = 0, where Aπθ is
comments.explanation: The condition `w^T A_{π^θ} w < 0` is intended to establish the negative definiteness (or negative stability) of the operator associated with the policy evaluation, which is crucial for proving convergence. However, stating it holds for `w = 0` is mathematically incorrect, as both sides evaluate to 0. It should clearly read `w ≠ 0` (or `w ∈ R^K \ {0}`). While this is likely a simple typographical error, as written it contradicts standard linear algebra conventions and breaks the logical flow of the assumption.

comments.title: Inconsistent step-sizes for the average reward estimate (µ)
comments.quote: where µt+1 = (1 − α) · µt + α · ¯rt+1 is the estimate for the average long term return, and α is the step-size for the actor step.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.4, the authors introduce `α` as the step-size for updating the average reward estimate `µ` in the context of the actor step. However, in Algorithm 1 (Critic Step), the update rule for `µ^i` uses `β` (the critic step-size) instead: `µ^i_{k,τ+1} ← (1 − β) · µ^i_{k,τ} + β · r^i_{k,τ+1}`. Using a shared or coupled step-size for the reward estimator across both actor and critic updates is not uncommon, but the explicit notation switch without clarification creates ambiguity. In average-reward AC analyses, the reward estimator's step-size often requires careful decoupling or separate notation to justify multi-time-scale convergence arguments. The authors should clarify whether `α` and `β` are intended to be identical, related, or if one update rule contains a typo.

comments.title: Potential index mismatch in the consensus update formula
comments.explanation: The surrounding text describes updating `w^i` via a 'weighted average of its local and neighboring agents’ parameters'. The formula provided, however, sums over neighbors `j ∈ N_i` but multiplies the weights `A_{ij}` with `˜w^i_k` (the agent's own updated parameter) rather than `˜w^j_k`. If taken literally, this reduces to `w^i_{k+1} = (\sum_j A_{ij}) \tilde{w}^i_k = \tilde{w}^i_k` (since `A` is row-stochastic), rendering the consensus step mathematically trivial and inconsistent with the text. This is highly likely an OCR error where `j` was misread as `i`. Regardless, the equation as printed is functionally broken and must be corrected to `˜w^j_k` for the algorithm to match its description.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency for the approximated value parameters

The notation `w(s)` is inconsistent with the established parameterization `V_θ(s; w^i) = φ(s)^T w^i` and could mislead a reader into thinking the parameters are state-dependent, which contradicts the linear function approximation setup.

It is not specified as a function of network properties (e.g., spectral gap, mixing time, `N`, or `η`) or the target accuracy `ε`. Without this specification, the bias from finite-time consensus cannot be bounded or guaranteed to be absorbed by the batch sizes and step sizes, making the algorithm impractical to reproduce or implement without heuristic guessing.
